exemplar.title: How does the voltage applied to the secondary solenoid affect the resultant magnetic field strength at the centre of a coaxial solenoid system when the primary solenoid voltage is kept constant?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the voltage applied to the secondary solenoid affect the resultant magnetic field strength at the centre of a coaxial solenoid system when the primary solenoid voltage is kept constant?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

files.fileName: oNetsanet Mengistie Physics HL IA draft.pdf

strengths: Data recording and processing are communicated clearly and precisely (complete tables, units, uncertainties, best-fit graph)
Regression and uncertainty propagation are carried out accurately
Graphical presentation allows quantitative interpretation of trends
weaknesses: Random uncertainty (trial spread) is not quantified
Vector nature of the B-field superposition is oversimplified in notation
Omissions in propagating all instrumental uncertainties and in stating uncertainty bounds for fit parameters
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates a high level of clarity and precision in data presentation and carries out accurate processing (averages, regression, propagated uncertainties). Some significant omissions remain (no random error analysis, incomplete uncertainty propagation), placing the work solidly in the 5â6 band but not wholly meeting the strict requirements for band 6, so a score of 5 is justified.

strengths: Research question embedded in a precise, real-world context (coaxial solenoids)
Procedure outlined clearly enough for broad reproducibility
Good control and alignment considerations, with clear apparatus illustration
weaknesses: Omission of key quantitative apparatus details (number of turns, sensor model, alignment tolerances)
Insufficient rationale for chosen voltage range and step size
Limited explanation of why methodological controls ensure sufficient data quality
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation situates the research question within a highly specific, appropriate context and outlines a step-by-step procedure that could be replicated with minor clarifications. Methodological considerations are described but not fully explained (e.g., no justification for choice of voltage range or tolerances). These qualities align with the upper half of the 3â4 band but fall just short of the full explanation required for band 6, warranting a score of 5.

strengths: Conclusion is fully consistent with the analysis and directly answers the research question
Strong linkage to accepted theory (AmpÃ¨reâs law, Biot-Savart, superposition) justifies the linear relationship
Quantitative result (slope) is quoted and tied clearly to the data
weaknesses: Could be strengthened by comparing the experimental gradient to a theoretical value from first principles
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is justified through a relevant comparison to accepted scientific context, fully consistent with the data analysis, and directly addresses the research question. It meets all descriptors for the 5â6 band with minimal areas for enhancement, warranting the top score of 6.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (instrument accuracy, alignment, environmental fields) are clearly described
Realistic and relevant improvements (better sensor, shielding, varying parameters) are proposed
weaknesses: Does not explain the relative impact of each limitation on the results
Suggested improvements are not linked quantitatively to error reduction or enhanced validity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation identifies concrete weaknesses and realistic, relevant improvements, placing it in the 3â4 band. However, the student stops short of explaining how each limitation quantitatively affects the outcome or how each improvement would reduce uncertainty, so the work does not yet reach the full explanation required for band 5. A score of 4 is therefore appropriate.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated and embedded in a **specific, real-world context**: a coaxial pair of solenoids used in electromagnetic devices.  The introduction explains the underlying theory (Biot-Savart law, super-position) and links it directly to the variables under study, so the reader understands *why* the relationship is worth exploring.  Because the coil geometry, variable, and measurement location (centre of the pair) are explicitly identified, the context is both appropriate and sufficiently narrow for a focused investigation.

Instrumental uncertainties for voltage (Â±0.01 V) and B-field (Â±0.001 mT) are stated; slope and intercept errors are propagated and tabulated, and systematic offsets are discussed.  However, random uncertainty from trial spread is ignored, and the origin-offset explanation lacks quantitative treatment.  These omissions keep the work short of âappropriateâ but show **substantial consideration**.

Methodological considerations are present (control of primary-coil voltage, coaxial alignment, sensor position, range of secondary voltages and four repeats).  Nevertheless, justification of *why* those ranges and repeats yield **sufficient** data is brief, and potential confounders such as heating, mutual inductance or sensor linearity are not examined.  The student therefore *describes* rather than *explains* the considerations, meriting the lower half of the 3â4 band.

Specific weaknessesâvoltmeter accuracy, magnetometer interference, solenoid misalignment, environmental fieldsâare **described**, but their *relative impact* on the gradient, intercept or scatter is not analysed.  Thus the strand fits the middle band, lower end.

The student lists apparatus, states measurement ranges (1â9 V) and gives a step-by-step outline, so another investigator **could replicate the broad procedure**.  However, essential quantitative detailsâexact number of turns, solenoid length, resistance, sensor model and orientationâare missing, leaving *some* ambiguity.  Overall, the description is adequate for reproduction with minor clarifications, matching the middle band.

Averages, linear regression, gradient extraction, and propagated uncertainties are all carried out correctly; numerical values are internally consistent with the raw data.  No significant mathematical errors are evident, indicating **accurate and appropriate processing**.

Improvements such as using a more sensitive magnetometer, shielding external fields, and varying the primary voltage are **relevant and realistic**, yet the student does not explain how each change would quantitatively reduce error or enhance validity.  The suggestions are therefore âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ, warranting a mid-band score.

Raw-data tables include units, uncertainties and four trials; processed averages and a labelled graph are provided.  Although the graph image is slightly cluttered, axis quantities, units and a best-fit line with equation are shown, allowing the trend to be read precisely.  Communication is therefore **both clear and precise** overall.

The conclusion is supported by a **relevant comparison to accepted theory**: AmpÃ¨reâs law, Biot-Savart, Ohmâs law and the super-position principle are cited and connected to the observed linearity.  This justification demonstrates clear understanding and meets the highest descriptor.

The student states a linear conclusion, quotes the gradient, and explicitly links it to the data and best-fit equation.  The conclusion is fully consistent with the analysed results and directly answers the research question, satisfying the top-band descriptor.

annotations.comment: The research question clearly situates the investigation in a specific coaxial solenoid context, defining both the independent and dependent variables precisely and referencing control of the primary voltage.

The methodology list names key apparatus but omits quantitative details (e.g., exact number of turns, solenoid length, resistance, sensor model/orientation), hindering full reproducibility. Add these specifications.
Background paragraphs rely heavily on nonâpeer-reviewed sources (Byjus, Toppr, âstudyâ). Improve citation consistency by using primary literature or standard textbooks with proper formatting.
The notation $$B_{net}=|B_1+B_2|$$ oversimplifies vector subtraction for opposing currents. Clarify the vector nature of superposition and specify directional signs rather than absolute value.
The objective is succinct and aligned with the research question, outlining clear aims to explore currentâfield relationships and demonstrate superposition practically.
Step 1 describes coaxial alignment using a lab stand but lacks tolerance or alignment accuracy. Specify alignment tolerances (e.g., Â±1 mm) to ensure reproducibility.
The experimental voltage range (1â9 V) is reasonable but unmotivated. Include a rationale for choosing these endpoints and step size to justify data sufficiency.
The inclusion of Figure 1 clearly illustrates the apparatus layout and sensor placement, supporting reproducibility and reader understanding.
Step 4 lists voltage variation but omits increment details (e.g., 1 V steps) and stabilization time. Specify step size and dwell time before measurement.
The raw data table presents four trials and includes sensor uncertainty for voltage; this precise recording enhances transparency and accuracy.
The table omits measurement uncertainty in B readings. Include the Â±0.001 mT sensor uncertainty alongside each average.
No random uncertainty (e.g., standard deviation or range) is calculated for trials. Add a spread metric to capture random error.
The caption for Table 1 concisely describes the plotted variables and units, reinforcing clarity in data reporting.
Figure 2 includes a bestâfit line and equation, effectively communicating the linear trend and allowing quantitative interpretation.
The graph image appears cluttered and lowâresolution, making axis ticks and data points hard to discern. Improve clarity by redrawing at higher resolution and simplifying style.
The standalone âBut.â is unclear and interrupts flow. Transition more smoothly by linking the argument to Ohmâs law context.
The derivation âHence, R â Vâ is incorrect. With constant R, Ohmâs law gives V â I. This logical error should be corrected.
The uncertainty analysis cites a slope error of Â±0.0005 but does not explain how this was obtained from the regression. Include mÂ±Îm directly from the fit statistics.
Instrumental uncertainties (voltmeter Â±0.01 V, magnetometer Â±0.001 mT) are listed but not propagated into the total uncertainty table. Incorporate these sources in ÎB_total.

The discussion of nonâzero intercept attributes possible sources qualitatively but omits quantitative estimates of their impact. Quantify the systematic offset from magnetometer bias or residual magnetism.
The conclusion succinctly restates findings, quoting slope and trend, and ties directly to data analysis, demonstrating full consistency.
Limitations are identified (e.g., voltmeter accuracy, solenoid alignment) but their relative impact on the measured gradient or intercept is not discussed. Explain which error dominates.
Proposed extensions (e.g., sensitive magnetometer, varying primary voltage, spatial mapping) are relevant but lack explanation of how each reduces uncertainty or enhances validity.
The regression equation is given without uncertainty bounds for m and c. Include the standard errors for gradient and intercept from the fit.
Comparing the experimental slope (0.0368 mT/V) to the theoretical value from $$rac{_0n}{R}$$ would strengthen the scientific context. Add this comparison.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.
